name: create-context-graph
maintainer: Neo4j Labs (community-supported, not official Neo4j product — maintained by Neo4j staff including William Lyon)
last_major_release: Active development on main branch (2026); versioned via CHANGELOG.md; no formal semver tag found as of Apr 2026. [uncertain]
activity: Actively maintained by Neo4j Labs staff in Apr 2026. Recent commits include: graceful Neo4j degradation, Cypher injection prevention, CrewAI deadlock fix, Claude Agent SDK model version config. 22 domain ontologies, 8 agent framework integrations. Stars/forks count not confirmed from search results. [uncertain about exact star count]
storage_backend: Neo4j (required, exclusive). All generated applications use Neo4j with vector indexes, fulltext indexes, GDS projections, and spatial Point properties. The neo4j-agent-memory library (v0.1.0) is the memory backend.
representation_primitive: Three-tier entity+edge knowledge graph (same as neo4j-agent-memory, which it wraps): (1) Short-term: (:Message) chains per session. (2) Long-term: POLE+O typed entity nodes with domain-specific subtype labels and typed relationship edges. Domain ontologies (YAML-driven) define additional node/edge types per industry vertical. (3) Reasoning: (:DecisionTrace) / (:TraceStep) nodes with full provenance — thought chains, tool calls, causal relationships. A single domain YAML file drives schema, models, agent tools, and Jinja2 template rendering for the entire generated application.
api_shape: CLI scaffolder (uvx create-context-graph or npx create-context-graph). Generates: FastAPI backend with domain-specific agent tools and Neo4j CRUD ops; Next.js + Chakra UI v3 frontend with streaming SSE chat, graph visualization, decision trace viewer, document browser; optional MCP server for Claude Desktop integration. Non-interactive mode with flags supported. Not itself a Python library or REST API — it is a project generator.
hosting: Self-host only for generated applications. Generated apps run locally (make start) or can be deployed to any server. Neo4j backend: Neo4j Desktop, Docker, or Neo4j AuraDB (free tier supported). No managed hosting of generated apps.
llm_dependency: Ingest: via neo4j-agent-memory, same configurable pipeline (spaCy/GLiNER2/GLiREL for local extraction, or LLM-based extractor). SaaS connectors (GitHub, Slack, Jira, etc.) run at data import time with optional LLM enrichment. Custom domain generation uses an LLM call to produce the domain YAML ontology from a plain English description. Retrieval: LLM-free (index-based search). Agent operation during chat: LLM required.
schema_extensibility: Yaml-config (domain YAML) + LLM-learns for custom domains. Each domain is defined by a YAML ontology specifying entity types, relationship types, constraints, indexes, GDS projections, agent tools, and sample queries. The 22 built-in domains are fixed templates. Custom domains generated via LLM from plain English description. The underlying POLE+O base from neo4j-agent-memory is fixed. Domain YAML drives Jinja2 template rendering — schema, API, and frontend are all derived from this single source of truth.
schema_richness: Per domain: each domain YAML defines 5-15+ domain-specific entity subtypes on top of the POLE+O base. Example — Healthcare domain: Patient, Provider, Diagnosis, Treatment, Procedure, Medication, Facility (7 domain subtypes + 5 POLE+O base = 12+ node types). Cross-domain base: 5 POLE+O types + Message, Session, Preference, ReasoningTrace, ReasoningStep = ~10 base types always present. Relationship types: domain-specific typed edges (e.g., :DIAGNOSED_WITH, :PRESCRIBED_BY, :WORKS_FOR) plus base POLE+O edges. Multi-label: yes (entity_type + subtype). Multi-graph: session_id + agent_id scoping. Total potential entity types for a single domain: 12-20+. 22 built-in domains cover diverse verticals but no software-development domain is present — Gimle would need a custom domain. Edge types per domain: typically 8-15 typed relationships. Can approach Gimle §5's 20+ entities / 15+ edges target with a custom software-development domain.
temporal_model: Snapshot with timestamps (inherited from neo4j-agent-memory). Messages and entities carry created_at. No bi-temporal validity windows on relationships. Reasoning memory (DecisionTrace/TraceStep) provides an append-only event log of agent decisions with timestamps — this constitutes a partial event-log model for the reasoning tier. The long-term entity graph has no contradiction resolution or validity-window semantics.
forgetting_policy: Semantic deduplication on entity ingest (inherited from neo4j-agent-memory: RapidFuzz + embedding similarity). No TTL eviction. No explicit forgetting. Session messages are logically scoped per session_id. Agent-driven deletion available via API.
ingest_latency_class: Real-time incremental for conversation messages (sub-second with local NLP pipeline). SaaS connector imports (GitHub, Slack, Jira, etc.) are batch-offline (run at setup time or on-demand, process full historical data over minutes-hours). Streaming extraction supported for large documents.
retrieval_modes: Hybrid (inherited from neo4j-agent-memory): vector semantic search + BM25 fulltext + graph traversal via custom Cypher. Generated agent tools include domain-specific Cypher queries (e.g., 'find all patients diagnosed with X and their treating providers'). Graph visualization in the Next.js frontend enables interactive traversal. Cross-tier context assembly via memory_get_context.
retrieval_agenticity: Static-lookup with domain-specific agent tools. Generated agent tools are hardcoded Cypher queries tailored to the domain ontology — they provide deep domain traversal but no dynamic query generation. The agent framework (PydanticAI, LangGraph, etc.) selects which tool to call. No Self-RAG or CRAG loop. Reasoning memory retrieval ('what did I decide last time about X') is available as a tool.
multi_project_namespace: agent_id and session_id scoping on nodes (inherited from neo4j-agent-memory). No formal project-level namespace. Multiple generated apps can share one Neo4j instance with isolation by agent_id. Less formal than Graphiti's group_id system.
multi_user_access: Per-agent namespace via agent_id. No RBAC. Multi-agent memory sharing via shared agent_id demonstrated in Neo4j blog ('When Your Agents Share a Brain'). No access control layer in the generated apps.
embedder_options: Inherited from neo4j-agent-memory: sentence-transformers by default (local, no API key), auto-upgrades to OpenAI text-embedding-ada-002 or text-embedding-3-small if OPENAI_API_KEY is present. Custom embedder configurable. [uncertain about exact default model]
llm_client_options: Agent framework choices at scaffold time determine LLM client: PydanticAI, Claude Agent SDK (claude-sonnet-4-5 by default, configurable via env var), OpenAI Agents, LangGraph, CrewAI, Strands, Google ADK, Anthropic Tools. All share same FastAPI backend; only agent implementation differs. LLM can be partially bypassed for memory extraction (local NLP pipeline) but agent chat responses always require LLM.
cost_privacy: With local NLP extraction (default): no data leaves host except to the agent LLM for chat. SaaS connector imports send data over HTTPS to the respective SaaS APIs (GitHub API, Slack API, etc.) but store results locally in Neo4j. Claude Code connector reads local session history with no API call. With LLM extraction enabled: conversation text sent to configured provider. Privacy profile: good for local/on-prem deployments with local NLP. SaaS connectors require API keys and data egress to those services.
code_artifact_linkage: Partial via SaaS connectors in generated apps. GitHub connector ingests: repositories, issues, PRs, commits as event data (mapped to Event/Object POLE+O types). No symbol-level (function/class) linkage, no AST-node linkage, no call-graph extraction. The GitHub connector provides issue-to-PR chain linkage via event graph but not to code symbols. All code artifact linkage is at the file/PR/issue level, not the symbol level.
code_analysis_features: GitHub connector provides: PR/issue/commit ingestion (file-level granularity). No call graph, diff analysis, symbol index, import resolution, or type-aware analysis. The generated Cypher tools can query 'all PRs related to this issue' or 'recent commits to this repo' but cannot link to function-level code entities. Cross-language: not supported. All code features are event-log style (what happened) not structural (how the code is organized).
memory_os_layer: Plaintext (external knowledge store). All three memory tiers are external graph-based retrieval stores. No parametric or activation memory layer. No cross-type migration.
self_improvement_mechanism: Reasoning memory (DecisionTrace/TraceStep) enables manual-curation-style self-improvement: agents can retrieve past decision traces for similar tasks and use them to inform new decisions. No automatic RL, LLM-reflection-based graph update, or online alias learning. The append-only reasoning trace record provides an audit trail for manual curation.
provenance_standard: Custom internal: DecisionTrace → TraceStep chains with links to entities and messages that informed each step. Google Workspace connector extracts resolved comment threads from Google Docs as decision traces, providing document-level provenance. No W3C PROV, OpenTelemetry, or MCP-native trace. Provenance is richer than neo4j-agent-memory alone due to the structured TraceStep-to-entity linkage.
benchmark_results: No published benchmarks for create-context-graph specifically. Tool is a scaffolder, not a standalone memory system — benchmark would need to target the generated application + neo4j-agent-memory. No third-party evaluations found as of Apr 2026. [uncertain]
strengths: 1. Fastest path to domain-specific context graph: 22 pre-built domain ontologies with full-stack scaffold (FastAPI + Next.js + Neo4j + agent tools) in under 5 minutes — provides an excellent reference architecture and schema design pattern for Gimle's N+1 work even if not adopted directly. 2. Domain YAML as single source of truth: the pattern of driving schema, API, frontend, and agent tools from a single YAML ontology is a directly transferable architectural principle for Gimle's graph schema management. 3. Native SaaS connectors including GitHub: GitHub issues/PRs/commits ingestion out of the box is the closest available open-source equivalent to what Gimle needs for its code context graph — steal the connector design pattern.
weaknesses: 1. No software-development domain built in: 22 domains cover healthcare, finance, real estate, etc., but not software engineering — a custom domain would need to be generated, without the benefit of battle-tested ontology design. 2. Scaffolder, not a library: create-context-graph generates a project; it does not provide reusable Python modules that Gimle could import. Adopting means either copying generated code or re-scaffolding when the tool updates. 3. Inherits neo4j-agent-memory temporal weakness: snapshot-only model (no bi-temporal validity windows) is insufficient for Gimle §5's requirement to query 'what was the architecture at time T'.
fit_with_gimle_section_5: Verdict: steal-specific-ideas — the domain YAML schema pattern, three-tier memory stratification, GitHub connector design, and reasoning trace provenance model are all directly applicable design inputs for Gimle N+1 schema work. Ideas to steal: (1) Domain YAML as schema source of truth: design Gimle's context graph schema as a single YAML/Pydantic ontology that drives Neo4j constraints, indexes, GDS projections, and agent tool Cypher queries. This prevents schema drift between DB and application layer. (2) Reasoning trace schema: adopt the DecisionTrace → TraceStep → Entity/Message provenance chain for Gimle's Decision/Iteration entities. Maps directly to §5's Decision and Iteration node types. (3) GitHub connector architecture: inspect the generated GitHub connector code to understand how PRs/issues/commits are mapped to POLE+O event graph — use as a starting point for Gimle's code extractor (currently the heartbeat extractor exists; a git-events extractor should follow this pattern). (4) Three-tier memory naming convention: short-term (session) / long-term (entity KG) / reasoning (decision traces) provides a clean framing for Gimle's memory architecture documentation. Schema design cue (domain YAML pattern): `domain: software_project; entities: [{name: Repository, pole_type: Object, properties: [name, url, primary_language]}, {name: Commit, pole_type: Event, properties: [sha, message, author, timestamp]}, {name: Symbol, pole_type: Object, properties: [fqn, kind, file_path, language]}]; relationships: [{type: MODIFIES, source: Commit, target: Symbol}, {type: DEFINED_IN, source: Symbol, target: File}]`. Do NOT adopt: the tool itself as a Gimle build dependency; the POLE+O-only temporal model for code artifact tracking; any generated frontend code.
